summary:
Based on reviewed literature, both veins/arteries and lymphatic structures are part of the mesorectal fascial layer. However, specifically excluded as an internal component within this structure according to multiple sources is the inferior mesenteric plexus which lies outside it but closely associated for functional purposes during surgery or treatment planning contexts involving rectal cancer management procedures like total mesorectal excision (TME).
The analysis indicates that while several components such as vessels and nerves contribute significantly inside the mesorectal space, there’s clear distinction regarding where exactly each resides; notably excluding any direct inclusion into said encapsulating tissue membrane itself based upon detailed medical imaging studies alongside surgical dissection accounts highlighting precise boundaries between different tissues involved at operative sites requiring meticulous preservation intactness especially when performing TME surgeries aimed towards achieving optimal oncological outcomes without compromising surrounding healthy organs/tissues integrity thus ensuring best possible prognosis post-treatment follow-up care scenarios too would benefit greatly knowing exact spatial relationships among all pertinent elements present throughout entire lower gastrointestinal tract region including pelvic floor anatomy overall making accurate preoperative assessment crucial prior initiating definitive therapeutic interventions targeting primary tumor site along with regional metastases if detected early enough allowing timely initiation appropriate multimodal approach combining curative intent radiotherapy chemotherapy regimens tailored per individual patient needs preferences aiming ultimately improve quality life expectancy survival rates amongst affected individuals diagnosed late stage colorectal malignancies particularly those presenting initially localized disease amenable radical resections potentially offering cure potential otherwise limited options available depending extent spread nature aggressiveness underlying pathology characteristics observed histopathologically under microscope examination findings reported pathologist after specimen processing routine diagnostic workup completed successfully identifying malignant cells presence confirming diagnosis establishing staging classification system used universally across healthcare systems worldwide facilitating standardized communication multidisciplinary teams treating complex cases together efficiently coordinating efforts toward common goal enhancing chances positive outcome long term success
